FACTS
The pertinent facts in the Armento letter are as follows: At
the behest of a developer, the city of south San Francisco created
the Shearwater redevelopEent Project (the I'project") at a site
encompassing 54 acres of dry land and 133 acres of water. 
A
master-planned, rnixed-use developnent is proposed.
The city orrns a six-acre parcel within the project area.
pursuant to an agreenent vith the developer, the city anticipates
se)"Iing or Ieasing this parcel to the developer. The details of
this tiansaction are being negotiated by the city manager, subject
to direction and ultiurate approval by the city council.
At the time the Armento Advice Letter was issued, there !,ere
a nunber of pending governmental decisions concerning the project'
The developer has requested that the city establish an assessment
district 
and a landscape and Iighting :naintenance district 
for
various financing PurPoses. other discussions and decj'sions
concerning the financial feasibility 
of the Project and the terms
of the ra1" o. lease of the city-owned proPerty were also pending.
Since the project would be constructed in phases, the j'ndividua]
aspects of tht project, such as the approval of all the buildings
lretait, 
office, hoteI, and, possibly condorniniurns) and the
specific design of the marina, will be subject to vari.ous apProval
processes.
Although the developer has retained its own realtor to act as
the agent for :narketing and leasj.ng the Shearwater site, any
brokei may bring a client directly to the developer and receive a
full comrnission for the Lease or sale of space in the project'
Councilmeruber Penna is a real estate broker and has been the owner
of Penna Realty for over 26 years. over 8o percent of his
business, which includes property tnanagement, real estate sales of
residential. commercial and industrial property, and real estate
appraising and consulting, is conducted in South San Francisco.
ctuncitmember Penna has not been invol.ved in any sales relating to
Shearwater.
During uarch 1990, counciLoe:aber Penna approached the
Shearuater developer and offered to do a presentati.on on the
Shearwater Project at the International Association of Realtors
(FIABCI) conference in Acapulco, Mexico. The Shearwater developer
authorized hin to make a presentation and supplied hirn r'rith
naterials. 
ApproxinateLy l.,oo0 brokers attended the conference.
If a broker cane forrrard as a result of the presentati.on and
engaged Councilrnember Penna to present an offer, Councilmernber
Penna would receive a comrnission. To date, no broker has come
forward in this matter.
Your request for reconsideration included additionaL and
modified facts which have been incorporated into this response.

ANALYS I S
The Political 
Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
state of california by inj.tiative in 1974. The purpose for the
conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the Act lras to ensure that
public of f icial.s. !.rhether elected or appointed, would perform
their duties in an inpartial. nanner, free from bias caused by
their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have suPPorted theD. (section 81001(b).) The Act
seeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of interest, it seeks
to prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest.
(r.Iitt v. I*{orrow, (L9771 7O Ca1.App.3d 817 , 823.')
In furtherance of this goal, section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official 
froru naking, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official 
position to


Our File No. A-90-4 99b
Page 5
influence a governmental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
As discussed in our first letter to your
office, a !'public official" 
is defined in Section 82048 and
Regulation 187oo as every natural person who is a menber, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local governEent agency.
rhis definition would include both redeveloPnent agency rnernbers
and nembers of the South San Francisco City CounciI. (Armento
Advice Letter, suDra. )
Please note that a conflict of interest arises with respect
to a soecific qovernmental decision. The Act does not restrict
council.menber Penna's private activity. 
Hor.rever, as wilI be
discussed below, where it is foreseeable that a governmental
decision r.ri11 affect an existing economic interest of the
councilmernber, even in the future, a conflict of interest arises
at the time of the decision.

official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
ernployee, or holds any position of nanagement.

The Thorner Opinion provides further guidance.  In Thorner′

Your reguest for reconsideration apPears to focus on this

However, j.t appears that your or.rn

pursuant to an agreenent vith the developer, the city anticipates

lretait,

ANALYS I S
The Political 
Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
state of california by inj.tiative in 1974. The purpose for the
conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the Act lras to ensure that
public of f icial.s. !.rhether elected or appointed, would perform
their duties in an inpartial. nanner, free from bias caused by
their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have suPPorted theD. (section 81001(b).) The Act
seeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of interest, it seeks
to prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest.
(r.Iitt v. I*{orrow, (L9771 7O Ca1.App.3d 817 , 823.')
In furtherance of this goal, section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official 
froru naking, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official 
position to

influence a governmental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
As discussed in our first letter to your
office, a !'public official" 
is defined in Section 82048 and
Regulation 187oo as every natural person who is a menber, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local governEent agency.
rhis definition would include both redeveloPnent agency rnernbers
and nembers of the South San Francisco City CounciI. (Armento
Advice Letter, suDra. )
Please note that a conflict of interest arises with respect
to a soecific qovernmental decision. The Act does not restrict
council.menber Penna's private activity. 
Hor.rever, as wilI be
discussed below, where it is foreseeable that a governmental
decision r.ri11 affect an existing economic interest of the
councilmernber, even in the future, a conflict of interest arises
at the time of the decision.

.on"".ni.g a gov"rnrn"ntal decision. the official

of official 
stationery.

6. Seqmentation of Decis ions
As qre stated above, since the decisions concerning the
